description:
You are a network-security engineer who has just found that a legacy
        iptables ruleset stored in 
        /home/user/network/firewall_rules.conf is exposing the
        Telnet service (TCP port 23).  
        
        Objective  
        ---------  
        1. Disable inbound Telnet without disturbing any other rule.  
        2. Keep a reproducible record of the change.  
        
        Required end-state  
        ------------------  
        1. A **backup** of the original ruleset must exist at  
           /home/user/network/firewall_rules.conf.bak (exact same
           byte-for-byte content the file had *before* you touched it).  
           
        2. The active ruleset file (/home/user/network/firewall_rules.conf)  
           must meet **ALL** of the following conditions *simultaneously*:  
           • The original “-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 23 -j ACCEPT” line is
             still present but is **commented out** by placing a literal ‘#’ as
             the very first character of that line.  
           • Immediately **below** that commented line you have added a new
             rule  
                   -A INPUT -p tcp --dport 23 -j DROP  
             (exact spacing as shown; no leading “#”).  
           • No other lines in the file are modified, reordered, removed or
             added.  
           
        3. Create /home/user/network/remediation.log whose content is **exactly**
           the six-line block shown below, with the numeric values filled in
           dynamically by your commands (do **not** hard-code them).  The
           automated grader will compare the file byte-for-byte, including
           newlines, so follow the template precisely:  
           
           BEGIN REMEDIATION LOG  
           BEFORE_ALLOW_RULES=<integer>  
           AFTER_ALLOW_RULES=<integer>  
           BEFORE_PORT23_POLICY=<ACCEPT_or_DROP>  
           AFTER_PORT23_POLICY=<ACCEPT_or_DROP>  
           END REMEDIATION LOG  
           
           Concrete expectations for this particular file:  
           • BEFORE_ALLOW_RULES must be the number of “-A INPUT … ACCEPT”
             lines that existed in the original file (should be 3).  
           • AFTER_ALLOW_RULES must be the number of “-A INPUT … ACCEPT”
             lines that remain after your fix (should be 2).  
           • BEFORE_PORT23_POLICY must be the literal word “ACCEPT”.  
           • AFTER_PORT23_POLICY must be the literal word “DROP”.  
           
        Constraints  
        ------------  
        • Accomplish everything in **no more than 10 shell commands** (simple
          shell built-ins like `cd`, `cp`, `grep`, `sed`, `echo` etc. count as
          commands).  
        • You have normal user privileges only.  
        • Work entirely inside /home/user/network; do not rely on external
          tools that are unlikely to exist in a minimal container.  
           
        Tips (optional, you may ignore)  
        --------------------------------  
        • Text-processing utilities (`grep -c`, `sed`, `awk`, etc.) are
          perfectly acceptable.  
        • The grader uses a byte-level diff, so watch out for trailing
          spaces, extra blank lines, or missing newline at EOF.
truth:
Initial files present when the scenario starts:

        1. Path: /home/user/network/firewall_rules.conf
           Content (exact, including blank lines):
# Simulated iptables-save output generated on 2024-01-01
*filter
:INPUT DROP [0:0]
:FORWARD DROP [0:0]
:OUTPUT ACCEPT [0:0]
-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT
-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 23 -j ACCEPT
-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 80 -j ACCEPT
-A INPUT -j DROP
COMMIT

           (File ends with a newline.)

        2. Path: /home/user/network/nmap_scan.txt  (not modified by the task)
           Content:
Starting Nmap 7.80 ( https://nmap.org ) at 2024-01-02 00:00 UTC
Nmap scan report for localhost (127.0.0.1)
Host is up (0.000098s latency).
Not shown: 997 closed ports
PORT   STATE SERVICE
22/tcp open  ssh
23/tcp open  telnet
80/tcp open  http

        Expected files after successful completion:

        1. /home/user/network/firewall_rules.conf.bak
           └─ Must be an **exact** copy of the original firewall_rules.conf
              (checksum identical).

        2. /home/user/network/firewall_rules.conf
           └─ Same as original except:
              a) Line “-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 23 -j ACCEPT” is now prefixed
                 with “#”.
              b) Immediately below that commented line a new line
                 “-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 23 -j DROP” has been inserted.
              c) No other modifications.

           Final expected full content:

# Simulated iptables-save output generated on 2024-01-01
*filter
:INPUT DROP [0:0]
:FORWARD DROP [0:0]
:OUTPUT ACCEPT [0:0]
-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 22 -j ACCEPT
#-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 23 -j ACCEPT
-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 23 -j DROP
-A INPUT -p tcp --dport 80 -j ACCEPT
-A INPUT -j DROP
COMMIT

        3. /home/user/network/remediation.log
           Exact content:
BEGIN REMEDIATION LOG
BEFORE_ALLOW_RULES=3
AFTER_ALLOW_RULES=2
BEFORE_PORT23_POLICY=ACCEPT
AFTER_PORT23_POLICY=DROP
END REMEDIATION LOG
           (File ends with a newline.)

        Permissions:
        • All files inherit default user RW permissions; no special modes required.
